question: How does reaction differ from autorun?

options.text: reaction doesn't fire the effect on initialization, autorun fires immediately
reaction tracks only the data function return value, autorun tracks all read observables
reaction provides access to the previous value, autorun doesn't
reaction works asynchronously, autorun works synchronously
options.explanation: Both reactions fire synchronously when observed data changes. The difference is how they determine dependencies and when they first trigger.

question: What happens if you don't call a reaction's disposer when a component unmounts?

options.text: Memory leak — the reaction keeps running and holding references to closures and the store
React will automatically clean up the reaction
options.explanation: React knows nothing about MobX reactions. Cleanup is the developer's responsibility via the useEffect cleanup function.

options.text: MobX will automatically disable the reaction after 60 seconds of inactivity
options.explanation: MobX has no timeout for reactions. autorun and reaction run indefinitely until the disposer is called.

options.text: Nothing bad — the reaction will simply stop working
options.explanation: The reaction will NOT stop working — it continues tracking observables and executing callbacks, causing memory leaks and potential errors.

question: When is it better to use when instead of reaction?

options.text: When you need to perform an action once when a condition is met
When you need to track changes continuously
options.explanation: For continuous tracking, use reaction or autorun. when fires once and automatically disposes itself.

options.text: When you need access to the previous value
options.explanation: when doesn't provide access to the previous value. Use reaction for that.

options.text: When the condition can become true multiple times
options.explanation: when fires only on the first time the condition is met. For repeated conditions, use reaction.

question: Why is it dangerous to modify observables inside autorun?

options.text: It can cause an infinite loop if autorun tracks the same observable it modifies
MobX forbids mutations inside reactions
options.explanation: MobX doesn't completely forbid mutations inside reactions, but with enforceActions: 'always' it will throw an error. The main danger is infinite loops.

options.text: Changes inside autorun are not tracked by MobX
options.explanation: Changes are tracked, and that's exactly why an infinite loop can occur: autorun modifies an observable, which triggers autorun again, which modifies the observable again.

options.text: autorun doesn't have access to the store's this
options.explanation: autorun is a regular function with closure, it has access to all variables from the outer scope.

question: Which form of when returns a Promise?

options.text: when with a single argument (only predicate, no callback)
when with two arguments (predicate + callback)
options.explanation: When passing two arguments, when returns a disposer (cancel function), not a Promise.

options.text: Both forms return a Promise
options.explanation: The callback form returns a disposer, while the no-callback form returns a Promise. These are two different APIs.

options.text: when never returns a Promise
options.explanation: when(predicate) without a callback returns a Promise that can be used with await.
